did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Topological Data Analysis (TDA) via persistent homology to extract topological features (persistence statistics and persistence curves) from skin lesion images. These features were then used as input for a linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to classify different types of skin lesions (MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC) from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing algorithms for segmenting and classifying skin lesions using Topological Data Analysis (TDA) and persistent homology. It describes specific methods (persistence statistics, persistence curves, linear SVM), reports results on the ISIC dataset (validation scores of 65.6%, 66%, 67.2%), and outlines a novel computational approach rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Topological approaches to skin disease image analysis
journal: 2018 IEEE International Conference on Big Data (Big Data)
brief_summary: This study developed algorithms for segmenting and classifying skin lesions using Topological Data Analysis (TDA) and persistent homology. The authors utilized the ISIC dataset, training on 10,015 images and validating on 1,932 images, with final scores reported on a holdout set of 1,512 images. For segmentation, a method based on the robustness of segmented regions achieved an average Intersection over Union (IOU) score of 0.6651. For classification, the researchers extracted topological features known as persistence statistics and persistence curves from persistence diagrams. These features were fed into a linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to distinguish between seven types of skin lesions. The classification results showed balanced accuracy scores of 65.6%, 67.2%, and 66.0% for different feature combinations on the validation set.

all_task_types.target: Binary mask isolating the diseased skin lesion from healthy skin
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a segmentation algorithm that outputs a binary mask M where M(x,y)=255 if the pixel is part of the lesion and 0 if it is healthy skin. The evaluation metric used was Intersection over Union (IOU), achieving an average score of 0.6651.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier with a 'one-against-one' strategy to classify skin lesions into seven distinct classes. The task is multi-class classification. Balanced accuracy scores of 65.6%, 66.0%, and 67.2% were reported on the validation set.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: The dataset used for the study, sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive. It contains dermatoscopic images of skin lesions collected from various sources.

use.training: Used for training the classification model (specifically, a subset of 5000 images was randomly chosen from the training set for the final classification experiments).
use.validation: Used for validation, with scores reported on this set.
use.testing: Used as a holdout set for reporting final scores.
use.other: A subset of 2595 images from the dataset was used to evaluate the segmentation algorithm's average IOU score.

statistical_methods_used.method: Persistence statistics (mean, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis, median, 25th/75th percentiles, interquartile range)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize the empirical distributions of midlife coordinates and normalized lifespans derived from persistence diagrams for feature extraction.
To measure the diversity of the lifespans of generators in persistence diagrams as a topological feature.
To classify skin lesions into seven categories using the extracted topological features.
To handle multi-class classification by decomposing the problem into binary classification tasks within the SVM framework.
To evaluate and compare the performance of different feature combinations on the validation set.
To evaluate the performance of the segmentation algorithm.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as balanced accuracy and Intersection over Union (IOU) on validation and holdout sets.

segmentation.notes: The average IOU score was calculated among 2595 images from the dataset.

Model 2.features: XYZ curves, XYZ stats, RGB stats

Model 3.features: XYZ curves, XYZ stats, RGB stats, HSV stats

classification.notes: Results reported on the validation set. The best 3 scores were obtained using a subset of the full training set (5000 images chosen randomly from the training set). The holdout set consisted of 1512 images.

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) using persistent homology
features: Persistence statistics (PS): means, standard deviations, skewness, kurtosis, medians, 25th/75th percentiles, interquartile ranges of midlife coordinates and normalized lifespans
Persistence curves (PC): Betti curves (β0, β1) and Entropy summary curves (E0, E1)
architecture_details.segmentation_algorithm: Robustness-based segmentation using persistent homology concepts (life interval of pixels)

training_details.training_source: Random subset of 5000 images chosen from the ISIC training set (10,015 images)

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Multi-class classification (7 classes: MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC)

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale conversion: RGB images were transformed into grayscale images by averaging color intensities (I*(x,y) = (R(x,y) + G(x,y) + B(x,y)) / 3).
Thresholding: Binary images were generated by thresholding grayscale images based on the average pixel value and a time step parameter T.
Connected component analysis: Used to isolate the main connected component of the lesion and remove tiny/noisy white components.
Convex hull computation: Applied to the main connected component to define the final segmentation mask.
conclusions: The study successfully developed algorithms for segmenting skin lesions and classifying seven types of skin lesions (MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC) using Topological Data Analysis (TDA) and persistent homology.
The segmentation algorithm, which captures the robustness of segmented regions, achieved an average Intersection over Union (IOU) score of 0.6651 on a subset of 2,595 images.
Classification performance was evaluated using balanced accuracy on a validation set, achieving scores of 65.6%, 66.0%, and 67.2% for different combinations of topological features.

The best classification score of 67.2% was obtained using persistence curves (XYZ color space) and persistence statistics (XYZ and RGB color spaces) as input to a linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier.

Persistence curves (PCs) and persistence statistics (PSs) are highlighted as simple features that do not require user-defined parameters, relying instead on the tuning of the attached machine learning algorithm.
These topological features provide intuitive shape summaries of the original image space, and the generalized nature of persistence curves allows for a rich library of usable curves.
The authors note that future work will focus on applying machine learning approaches specifically to persistence curves.
important_remarks: The classification model was trained on a random subset of only 5,000 images from the ISIC training set (total 10,015), rather than the full available training data, which may limit model capacity and generalizability.

The reported balanced accuracy scores (65.6%, 66.0%, 67.2%) are relatively low for a multi-class classification task involving seven distinct skin lesion types, suggesting limited diagnostic utility compared to state-of-the-art deep learning methods.

The study lacks external validation; results are reported solely on the internal ISIC 2018 validation and holdout sets, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the performance of different feature combinations or against baseline methods.

The segmentation algorithm is parameter-free regarding topological features but relies on a fixed threshold parameter T=50 and a heuristic formula for T0, which may not be robust across diverse lesion morphologies.
The paper does not report per-class performance metrics, making it impossible to assess bias or performance disparities among specific lesion types (e.g., melanoma vs. benign nevi).

The approach relies on traditional machine learning (Linear SVM) with hand-crafted topological features rather than end-to-end deep learning, which may be less effective at capturing complex visual patterns in dermatoscopic images.
